title: What Miss Edith Saw from Her Window
content:
آ آ آ آ Our windowï؟½s not much, though it fronts on the street;
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a fly in the pane that gets nothinï؟½ to eat;
آ آ آ آ But itï؟½s curious how people think itï؟½s a treat
آ آ آ آ For me to look out of the window!

آ آ آ آ Why, when company comes, and theyï؟½re all speaking low,
آ آ آ آ With their chairs drawn together, then some one says, ï؟½Oh!
آ آ آ آ Edith dear! thatï؟½s a good child now run, love, and go
آ آ آ آ And amuse yourself there at the window!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Or Bob thatï؟½s my brother comes in with his chum,
آ آ آ آ And they whisper and chuckle, the same words will come.
آ آ آ آ And itï؟½s ï؟½Edith, look here! Oh, I say! what a rum
آ آ آ آ Lot of things you can see from that window!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And yet, as I told you, thereï؟½s only that fly
آ آ آ آ Buzzing round in the pane, and a bit of blue sky,
آ آ آ آ And the girl in the opposite window, that I
آ آ آ آ Look at when she looks from her window.

آ آ آ آ And yet, Iï؟½ve been thinking Iï؟½d so like to see
آ آ آ آ If what goes on behind her, goes on behind me!
آ آ آ آ And then, goodness gracious! what fun it would be
آ آ آ آ For us both as we sit by our window!

آ آ آ آ How weï؟½d know when the parcels were hid in a drawer,
آ آ آ آ Or things taken out that one never sees more;
آ آ آ آ What people come in and go out of the door,
آ آ آ آ That we never see from the window!

آ آ آ آ And that night when the stranger came home with our Jane
آ آ آ آ I might see what I heard then, that sounded so plain
آ آ آ آ Like when my wet fingers I rub on the pane
آ آ آ آ (Which they wonï؟½t let me do on my window).

آ آ آ آ And Iï؟½d know why papa shut the door with a slam,
آ آ آ آ And said something funny that sounded like ï؟½jam,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And then ï؟½Edith where are you?ï؟½ I said, ï؟½Here I am.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ah, thatï؟½s right, dear, look out of the window!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ They say when Iï؟½m grown up these things will appear
آ آ آ آ More plain than they do when I look at them here,
آ آ آ آ But I think I see some things uncommonly clear,
آ آ آ آ As I sit and look down from the window.

آ آ آ آ What things? Oh, the things that I make up, you know,
آ آ آ آ Out of stories Iï؟½ve read and they all pass below.
آ آ آ آ Ali Baba, the Forty Thieves, all in a row,
آ آ آ آ Go by, as I look from my window.

آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s only at church time; other days thereï؟½s no crowd.
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t laugh! See that big man who looked up and bowed?
آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s our butcher I call him the Sultan Mahoud
آ آ آ آ When he nods to me here at the window!

آ آ آ آ And that man heï؟½s our neighbor just gone for a ride
آ آ آ آ Has three wives in the churchyard that lie side by side.
آ آ آ آ So I call him ï؟½Bluebeardï؟½ in search of his bride,
آ آ آ آ While Iï؟½m Sister Anne at the window.

آ آ آ آ And what do I call you? Well, hereï؟½s what I do:
آ آ آ آ When my sister expects you, she puts me here, too;
آ آ آ آ But I wait till you enter, to see if itï؟½s you,
آ آ آ آ And then I just open the window!

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Dear child!ï؟½ Yes, thatï؟½s me! Oh, you ask what thatï؟½s for?
آ آ آ آ Well, Papa says youï؟½re ï؟½Povertyï؟½s self,ï؟½ and whatï؟½s more,
آ آ آ آ I open the window, when youï؟½re at the door,
آ آ آ آ To see Love fly out of the window!ï؟½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
